chunk_summary: This section discusses protocols for bolus, continuous, and slow gravity delivery in enteral nutrition, emphasizing patient-specific approaches, safety measures, and interruption management.
learning_objectives: Understand the differences between bolus, continuous, and slow gravity feeding protocols in enteral nutrition.
Learn how to safely implement bolus feeding with controlled delivery times and volume limits.
Identify strategies to manage interruptions and prevent underfeeding in enteral nutrition.
Recognize contamination risks and safe hang times for different feeding systems and climates.
key_facts: Bolus feeding should be delivered over 20–30 minutes to reduce emesis and aspiration risks.
Continuous feeding is preferred for impaired gastric emptying, fluid restrictions, or postpyloric feeding (e.g., jejunal feeds).
Slow gravity feeding can be used as a semi-continuous method over 30–60 minutes when pumps are unavailable.
Open systems have shorter safe hang times than closed systems; blenderized feeds often require hang times ≤2 hours.
Protocols should include a restart plan after interruptions to prevent underfeeding, with daily checks of delivered versus prescribed nutrition.
reference_quote: Intermittent bolus gastric feeding is usually preferred for stable children because it is more physiologic, easier to schedule, and easier for families to replicate at home. (Source: ESPGHAN Practical Approach to Paediatric Enteral Nutrition)
document_key_topics: Safe delivery methods for enteral nutrition
